rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Patients received 60 sessions of tDCS treatment over 12 weeks, with follow-up assessments conducted 3 months after the primary endpoint.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Attexis and tDCS (verum); Arm 2: Attexis and placebo-tDCS (sham).
rows.collected_data: Compliance (number of days of tDCS treatment), efficacy measures (ASRS-v1.1 total score, subscale attention, subscale hyperactivity), stability of effects (change in ASRS-v1.1 sumscore), usability (UEQ sumscore).

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received access to attexis in addition to treatment as usual (TAU). Control group received access to TAU only.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: ADHD Symptom Severity (ASRS v1.1, score range 0-72, higher scores indicate worse outcome) assessed at 3 months. Secondary Outcomes: Depressive Symptomatology (PHQ-9, score range 0-27, higher scores indicate worse outcome), Self Esteem (RSES, score range 0-30, higher scores indicate better outcome), Functioning (WSAS, score range 0-40, higher scores indicate worse outcome), Health-Related Quality of Life (AQoL-8D, score range 0-100, higher scores indicate better outcome), ADHD Symptom Severity (ASRS v1.1) assessed at 6 months.

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received attexis + TAU; control group received TAU alone.
rows.collected_data: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale total score, functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, health-related quality of life.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements across all secondary outcomes, effects stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Access to the full version of the App Cara Care für Reizdarm. Arm 2: Standard of Care and no application of the App Cara Care für Reizdarm or an alternative App.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Disease-specific symptom severity (IBS-SSS), Quality of life (IBS-QOL), Work and activity impairment (WPAI-IBS), Health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16). Secondary outcomes: Perception of anxiety (GAD-7), Perception of depressiveness (PHQ-9). Data collected using standardized, validated health questionnaires.

rows.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes were measured at 4 time points: day 0, 30, 60, and 90.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 90-day personalized exercise therapy via the DiGA companion patella app. Control arm: Conventional analog treatment by physiotherapists as per statutory health insurance agreements.
rows.collected_data: Pain reduction measured on the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) and knee joint function improvement measured on the Kujala Score at 4 time points (day 0, 30, 60, 90).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 90 days, and primary and secondary outcomes are measured at day 90.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group receives the DiGA companion® shoulder for 90 days, including personalized exercise therapy and education. Control group receives standard physiotherapy care for 90 days, typically 1 to 3 prescriptions with 6 units each.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Shoulder-specific pain reduction measured with Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) after 90 days. Secondary outcomes: Shoulder-specific functional improvement measured with Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI) after 90 days, and number of days of incapacity for work due to illness within the survey period measured via self-reporting at day 90.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted nine weeks, and follow-up assessments were conducted six months later.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received immediate access to the web-based intervention Deprexis, while the control arm was a wait-list group that received access to Deprexis after six months.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for severity of depressive symptoms, WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF), Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (QIDS), Core Self-Evaluations Scale (CSES), Suicidal Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Hamburg MS Quality of Life Questionnaire (HALEMS).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention arm received access to the computerized self-help program for depression (Deprexis) for 8 weeks. The control arm was a wait-list control, where participants waited 8 weeks before accessing the program.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16) administered every 2 weeks during treatment; Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) completed pre- and post-treatment; Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS) completed pre- and post-treatment; Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ) completed pre- and post-treatment; Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ) completed pre-treatment; Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ) completed pre-treatment; Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) completed pre- and post-treatment.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Unguided self-help group received a web-based self-help program without therapist support. Guided self-help group received the same program with weekly therapist e-mail feedback. Wait-list group received access to the program after 10 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory - II (BDI-II), Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI), Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (64-item IIP), WHOQOL-BREF, CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Symptoms of depression and other aspects were assessed over a one-year period. Controls received online self-help after the one-year follow-up.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received online self-help (Deprexis) with and without email support. The control group was a waiting-list control.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Personal Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary Outcomes: Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; PHQ-9 at 6 months and 12 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received the web-based intervention Deprexis, consisting of ten online modules with cognitive-behavioral techniques. The control arm was a wait-list group that received access to Deprexis after eight weeks.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for severity of depressive symptoms; WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) for quality of life.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed three months after randomization; secondary outcomes assessed at randomization, end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), and six months after randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = deprexis® group, C = Information group
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group used deprexis® for 90 days as an additional treatment during inpatient stay and as aftercare intervention. Placebo comparator group received weekly online information about depression for 90 days in addition to inpatient treatment and as aftercare intervention.
rows.collected_data: Reduction of depression measured with Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II) and Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9); reduction of anxiety measured with Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7); reduction of dysfunctional depression-related cognitions measured with Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS); improved self-esteem measured with Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE); improved quality of life measured with European Health Interview Survey Quality of Life-8 (EUROHIS-QOL 8); improved working ability measured with Work Ability Index (WAI); acceptance and utilization of deprexis® and placebo information measured with self-devised questionnaires; degree of childhood traumas measured with Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ); degree of structural deficits measured with OPD-Structure Questionnaire (OPD-SFK); therapeutic alliance measured with Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ); course of mood, depressiveness, and utilization of units; utilization of other treatments after inpatient treatment; satisfaction with inpatient treatment; willingness to pay; remission from depression (BDI score below cutoff of 13 and Reliable Change Index).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a stepped care model (SCM) with treatments varying by depression severity, including active monitoring, bibliotherapy, online self-help, outpatient psychotherapy, psychiatric treatment, and combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment. The control group received treatment as usual from general physicians or other health service providers.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-D-9 depression scale, cost-benefit ratio in QALYs, response/remission/relapse (PHQ-D-9), quality of life (EQ-5D, SF-12), health service utilization and medication consumption, Rosenberg self-esteem scale, Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), PHQ-D anxiety scale, PHQ-D panic symptoms, PHQ-D somatisation, GAD-7 for general anxiety disorder.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: an internet-delivered psychosocial treatment for depressive symptoms and related functional impairment. Treatment-as-Usual: Access to standard non-study care.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report; QIDS-SR-16) to measure depressive symptom severity (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16). World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS) to assess functional disability across 7 domains (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16). The Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) to measure symptom-related disability (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants will be assessed at baseline (T0), mid-intervention (4 weeks, T1), post-intervention (8 weeks, T2), and follow-up (3 months post-intervention, T3).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 160 (80 in each experimental group) and C = 80
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group: Online self-help program for depression (Deprexis) for 8 weeks. Second Experimental group: Online self-help program (Deprexis) plus peer-support via an online forum for 8 weeks. Control group: Waitlist control group with access to the program after 8 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Depressive symptoms measured with PHQ-9 at post-treatment (8 weeks). Secondary Outcomes: Reward responses assessed using Fribourg Reward Task, SHAPS, BIS/BAS at baseline, mid-intervention (4 weeks), post-intervention (8 weeks), and follow-up (3 months). Perceived social support measured with PSS at baseline and post-treatment. Exploratory Outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7), self-efficacy (GSES), quality of life (SF-12), self-esteem (RSE), stress (PSS), trauma (CTQ-SF), pain (SSS-8), PTSD symptoms (PCL-5), client satisfaction (CSQ), usability (SUS), and working alliance.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at 3 months. Long-term online follow-up was conducted at 6 and 12 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 186 (Deprexis and DeprexisPlus combined) and C = 93 (Waitlist Control)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The trial had three arms: (1) Deprexis group received access to the web-based Deprexis program, an online tool based on principles of cognitive behavioral therapy; (2) DeprexisPlus group received the web-based Deprexis program plus scheduled email contact (1x/week); (3) Waitlist Control group waited for access to the Deprexis program for 6 months, after which they received full access to Deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (Month 0 to Month 3). Secondary Outcomes: WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) with 4 subscales (Physical, Psychological, Social Relationships, Environmental) (Month 0 to Month 3); Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS) with 2 subscales (Physical and Psychological) (Month 0 to Month 3); Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC) with total score and 2 subscales (Motor and Cognitive) (Month 0 to Month 3); Chalder Fatigue Scale (Month 0 to Month 3). Other Outcomes: Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS) with 3 subtests (SMDT total score, CVLT-II learning score and delayed recall, BVMT-R learning score and delayed recall) (Month 0 to Month 3); Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R) (Month 3); Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview, clinician rating, Version 5.0.0, dichotomous variable "Major Depressive Episode, Current (yes/no)" (Month 0 to Month 3).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: Online self-help; CAU: Care as usual
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items (PHQ-9) measured from Baseline to post (3 months). Secondary Outcomes: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms - Self-Report (QIDS-SR16), Generalized Anxiety Disorder - 7 (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire - 15 items (PHQ-15), Short Form Health Survey - 12 (SF-12) measured at Baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group used the Deprexis program for 12 weeks, while the control group did not have access to the program.
rows.collected_data: Depression severity: PHQ-9; Quality of life: SF12 and EQ-5D; Social functioning: WSAS; Cost data from DAK (excluding ambulant costs); Satisfaction with DAK services and acceptance of Deprexis program.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received an 8-week internet-based program ('Deprexis') focused on depression treatment, including modules based on cognitive-behavioral therapy. Wait-list control group did not receive additional treatment during the intervention period but gained access to 'Deprexis' after post-assessment.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 (depressive symptoms), SOGS (pathological gambling), PG-YBOCS (severity of gambling symptoms), GAD-7 (anxiety levels), WSQ (common mental disorders, baseline only), subjective appraisal of intervention (post-assessment only). Demographic and psychopathological information, clinical anamnesis (e.g., current treatments, medication use, psychiatric diagnoses) were also collected.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline (T1) and after 12 weeks (T4) for all groups. Additional interim assessments were conducted at 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3) for the supported intervention group (IG-A).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: IG-A: Supported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® and brief therapeutic consultations at 4, 8, and 12 weeks. IG-B: Unsupported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® only. CG: Waitlist control group receiving no intervention during the 12-week period but gaining access to DiGA deprexis® afterward.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Reduction in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9), anxiety symptoms (GAD-7), and general psychological distress (SCL-90-R). Secondary outcomes: Acceptance (e.g., satisfaction, perceived fit), usage intensity, adherence, dropout rates, reasons for dropout, subjectively perceived effects and burdens. Exploratory outcomes: Comparison of supported vs unsupported DiGA use, moderator and predictor variables (e.g., sociodemographics, baseline symptoms, attitudes toward digital health applications), and evaluation of DiGA's relevance as a bridging intervention.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus web-based online platform (deprexis). Control group: Psychotherapy without Deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) somatic symptom module, Short-Form Health Survey-12 (SF-12), Questionnaire for the evaluation of psychotherapeutic progress (FEP-2), Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ), Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Psychological empowerment scale (adapted version), Depression diagnosis by licensed psychotherapists.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at three time points: before receiving access to the treatment, after 10 weeks, and after 6 months.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One group received immediate access to the web-based treatment deprexis, while the other group received access after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Depression levels by BDI-II, clinical depression by Patient Health Questionnaire and SCID interview; stress perception by Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ); social support by ENRICHD Social Support Inventory (ESSI); quality of life by WHOQOL-BREF; health behavior; client satisfaction by Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received access to the Deprexis® program, consisting of ten interactive modules completed over nine weeks. The control group did not receive access to the program during the first phase.
rows.collected_data: German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation), subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items. Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis; control group was a waitlist.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS) assessing symptoms such as sadness, indecision, insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved sadness and indecision, which were associated with changes in other symptoms like early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. Most symptoms showed decreases compared to the waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons, except for agitation and early insomnia. Six somatic symptoms were not impacted by Deprexis compared to the waitlist.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Post-discharge, patients were followed by structured telephone interviews up to study-endpoint, i.e., 12 weeks after deprexis-initiation.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: TAU group received treatment-as-usual, while TAU-PLUS group received treatment-as-usual plus guided deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Beck-Depression-Inventory-II (BDI-II), Hamilton-Depression-Scale, Clinical-Global-Impression-Severity, WHO-Well-Being-Index, Helping-Alliance-Questionnaire, working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, utilization of post-hospital care after discharge.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, TAU-PLUS patients showed significant improvement in BDI-II scores (p=.03, d=-.73), greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70), earlier discharge from inpatient treatment (p=.003), lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01), and fewer re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures and alliance with therapists were not significantly different between groups. Working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Follow-up assessments were conducted at three months and nine months post-treatment.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a 12-week online intervention (deprexis), while the control group received a control condition.
rows.collected_data: Statutory health insurance costs (primary outcome), depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning (secondary outcomes). Self-reports were collected at baseline, post-treatment, and three-months and nine-months follow-up.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a 32% reduction in statutory health insurance costs (from 3139€ to 2119€ per year) compared to a 13% reduction in the control group. The intervention group also had significantly greater reductions in depression severity and impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants in the control group received delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks, and the intervention group had access to Deprexis for 90 days.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days. Control group received treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM), perceived self-efficacy measure, login frequency, dropout rates, satisfaction levels.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the intervention group showed significant improvement in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9; Cohen d=0.80; P<.001), general psychological state (CORE-OM; Cohen d=0.82; P<.001), and perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission rates were 21% in the intervention group compared to 7% in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the intervention group, and participants reported medium to high satisfaction with Deprexis.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received an 8-week course of Deprexis immediately; control group was waitlisted for 8 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR), interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, depression-related disability, symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for secondary outcomes such as interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy alone.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 12 weeks; secondary outcomes included anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life at 12 weeks and six months follow-up; working alliance assessed at six and 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks, with medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07-.55). No negative side effects, such as a lower working alliance, were observed in the blended format.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received 9 weeks of immediate-program-access as an add-on to treatment-as-usual. Control group received 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for depression severity as the primary outcome measure. Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) for social functioning as the secondary outcome measure. Measurements were taken at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in depression severity (BDI) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94) and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95) at 9 weeks. Improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses showed significant effects on depression (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55) and social functioning (WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). Higher percentage of intervention group experienced significant reduction in depression symptoms (BDI: OR = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, CI 95% = 2.3 - 130). Over 80% of users found the program helpful.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy only.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 3 months (primary outcome). Secondary outcomes include changes in anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, working alliance, and feasibility/acceptability of the treatment condition. All outcomes assessed again at 6 months post-randomization.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants received TILS or sham/placebo once a week for 4 weeks, starting after the first 2 weeks of Deprexis.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis combined with TILS; control group received Deprexis combined with sham/placebo.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) to measure depressive symptoms.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The sham group showed a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores, while the TILS group showed a 56% reduction, representing an additional 30% reduction in depressive symptoms with TILS. No adverse effects were reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were re-assessed after 8 weeks.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the online depression-focused intervention 'Deprexis'; control group was a wait-list control.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS), South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for gambling-related symptoms.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive and gambling-related symptoms compared to the control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. Deprexis was particularly beneficial for individuals with high baseline gambling-related symptoms and those gambling due to loneliness.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Intermediary and long-term assessments were conducted over 36 months.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the Internet-based intervention (Deprexis), while the control group received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Symptom-specific effects of depression, quality of life aspects (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), baseline scores predicting symptom reduction, intermediary and long-term assessments, network intervention analysis.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Internet interventions for depression (Deprexis) are effective in the short term and exert long-term effects. Effects are symptom-specific, directly affecting some symptoms (e.g., worthlessness, fatigue) and indirectly affecting others (e.g., depressed mood, concentration, activity level). Patients with specific problems are likely to benefit more.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week web-based cognitive behavioral intervention (deprexis) in addition to care as usual. Control group received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, direct health care costs. Outcomes were assessed via patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between groups. Outpatient treatment costs showed numeric differences, with psychotherapeutic treatment costs decreasing in the intervention group (-16.8% (€80)) and increasing in the control group (+14.7% (€60)) (tdf=685=2.57; p=0.008).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the Deprexis online cognitive behavioural therapy programme; control group was a waitlist control.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) scores, occurrence of depressive symptoms above clinical cutoff (BDI >13), occurrence of suicidal ideation.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group, with a mean group difference of -4.02 points (95% CI -7.26 to -0.79, p=0.015, effect size d=0.53). Worsening of depressive symptoms above the clinical cutoff occurred in 7% of the control group and 0% of the Deprexis group. No adverse events related to suicidal ideation were noted.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis; control group received care as usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Depression symptoms pre and post intervention, patient characteristics, self-rating screening for mental disorders, attitudes towards online interventions, quality of life, adherence metrics (usage time and number of sessions).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher adherence to the program predicted greater symptom reduction in depressive symptoms over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14). Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Receiving guidance had a stronger association with symptom reduction (β = .27 and .26).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis in addition to care as usual (CAU), while control group received CAU alone.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms. Measurements were taken at baseline, 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores, indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. No sociodemographic variables were found to moderate treatment outcomes. Deprexis was equally beneficial across all subgroups.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group accessed the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual, while the control group had access to care-as-usual only.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, Web Screening Questionnaire for anxiety-related comorbidities (generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis. The moderating effect was statistically significant (p < .05) but accounted for less than 1% of the variance in depressive symptom changes and showed limited reproducibility.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I), Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8), Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI)
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The results suggested a two-factor solution, with a very good model fit and evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and the sub-scales. The WAI-I was found to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.collected_data: Baseline variables, early change patterns in the first four weeks, latent classes (early response after registration, early response after screening, early deterioration), predictors associated with remission onset, Stepwise Cox Proportional Hazard Multiple Regression analysis.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Early response after registration and early response after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission compared to early deterioration. Early improvement in depression symptoms predicted long-term remission.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 biweekly during treatment, change-dropout patterns, reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Participants showing high impairment, improvement and low dropout probability (C3, N = 134) had the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up. Rapid improvement may lead to dropout as a result of a perceived 'good enough' dosage of treatment.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis (online intervention for depression) and control group received care as usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Compared to Millennials, Boomers used the intervention significantly more often (d = 0.45) and for a longer duration (d = 0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d = 0.14). No significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. Post-assessment between-group effect sizes for Millennials and Boomers were d = 0.26 and d = 0.39, respectively, and were stable at follow-up (d = 0.37 and d = 0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and comparably effective among Millennials and Baby Boomers.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 1013 (Deprexis® intervention group) and C = care as usual
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the internet-based intervention Deprexis®, while the control group received care as usual. Both groups were permitted to use any type of treatment.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) assessing nine depressive symptoms, treatment condition, changes in symptoms, network estimation techniques.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Seven of nine depressive symptoms showed larger improvements in the intervention group compared to care as usual, with effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31. No significant differences were found for the other two symptoms. Network estimation suggested that four symptom-specific effects were direct, while three were indirect. Exploratory analyses indicated greater effectiveness for participants with higher scores on the four directly affected symptoms.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period lasted 3 months.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the Internet-based program deprexis, while the control group received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions, efficacy of the program deprexis, changes in attitudes from pre to post intervention, perceived lack of personal contact, usage time.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014), while negative attitudes were associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). The magnitude and direction of attitude change during the trial were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care, while the control group received care as usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for self-rated depression severity, assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). Post-assessment between-group effect size in favor of the intervention was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64), stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). Rate of participants experiencing minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%) with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Standard psychotherapy group (control) and augmented therapy group (intervention) with adjunctive Internet intervention (Deprexis).
rows.collected_data: Baseline assessments, post-treatment assessments, 6-month follow-up assessments, measures of depression symptoms, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Combined treatment led to greater reduction in symptoms of depression (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). No intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. Same pattern observed at 6-month follow-up with increased rates of clinical improvement.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) was assessed regularly over twelve months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) + usual care, C = care as usual alone
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care, while the control group received care as usual alone.
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) was assessed regularly over twelve months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates were significantly higher in the intervention group (HR 1.31; p = 0.009). The intervention was more effective in participants not taking antidepressant medication (HR 1.88; p < 0.001). PHQ-change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (p < 0.001), with the between-group effect decreasing from d = 0.36 at three months to d = 0.13 at twelve months (group by time interaction: p < 0.001).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) + usual care, C = care-as-usual alone
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care, while the control arm received care-as-usual alone.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for self-rated depression severity, demographic and clinical parameters, Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction=0.28, p=0.84). The intervention was effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Observation period of 3 months with data collection at 4 time points: baseline (start of therapy), 2 follow-up visits (approximately 3 and 6 weeks after baseline), and a final examination (approximately 12 weeks after baseline).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants used the deprexis®24 online psychotherapy program, with or without antidepressive comedication.
rows.collected_data: Short version Montgomery Asberg Depression Scale (svMADRS), Clinical Global Impression-Severity/Improvement (CGI-S / CGI-I), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS), Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-4), questionnaires for compliance and internet usage, and standardized documentation of treatment effects and safety-related incidents. Data were collected at four time points: baseline, 3 weeks, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline as well as at weeks 3, 6, 9 and 12.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received 12-week access to the self-management intervention (deprexis) in addition to usual care. Guidance could optionally be offered by the treating physician.
rows.collected_data: Clinician-rated short version of the Montgomery Asberg-Depression Scale (svMADRS) and the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms. Usage data of the intervention (e.g., number of sessions) was also tracked.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The severity of depressive symptoms decreased significantly over the observation period: svMADRS scores reduced from 29.72 (SD = 10.03) to 15.73 (SD = 9.74) with Cohen's d = 1.42 (95% CI 0.08-2.76), and PHQ-9 scores reduced from 15.20 (SD = 5.03) to 8.77 (SD = 5.03) with Cohen's d = 1.29 (95% CI 0.60-1.97). Most patients (83.6%) used the intervention at least once, with a mean of 18.05 sessions (SD = 11.33).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The programme lasts for 8 weeks, with pre-post training comparisons immediately after the training and 3 months later.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group (IG) received access to the online training tool for 8 weeks. Control group (CG) was a wait-list group that received information about entering the programme after the study.
rows.collected_data: 1. Fatigue: Chalder Fatigue Scale (primary outcome), FSMC (Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions). 2. Anxiety and depression: Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS). 3. Quality of life: Hamburg Quality of Life Questionnaire in MS (HAQUAMS). 4. Cognitive self-assessment: Multiple Sclerosis Neuropsychological Screening Questionnaire (MSNQ). Exploratory measures: Cognitive and behavioural responses to symptoms questionnaire, Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire, Coping Self Efficacy Scale (CSES), Frenchay Activity Index, Revised NEO Personality Inventory.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week online fatigue management programme (ELEVIDA) based on CBT and mindfulness; control group was a waitlist control.
rows.collected_data: Chalder Fatigue Scale scores assessed at baseline, week 12 (postintervention), and week 24 (follow-up).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores were observed in the ELEVIDA group at week 12 (between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53), with effects sustained at week 24 (between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080).

The control group received care-as-usual (CAU), which includes unrestricted access to routine care options such as general practitioners, pain physicians, psychotherapeutic treatment, drug therapy, physical therapy, and exercise therapy. After the 3-month data collection, control group participants could access the online program if desired.

Data were collected at baseline, post-treatment (3 months), and follow-ups at 6 and 12 months.

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS (knee injury and osteoarthritis outcome score). Secondary outcomes: TAMPA scale (kinesiophobia), numeric VAS (subjective pain level, subjective function, subjective patient satisfaction), physical therapy/lymphatic drainage needed (yes/no), ability to work (yes/no), patient-relevant structural and procedural improvements (pSVV), thigh circumference, lower leg circumference, patellar center, angular measurements (range of motion), stability test (anteroposterior stability of the knee joint), coordination, quick strength, agility (end of study only), exploratory endpoints on app use (intervention group only) via custom-developed questions.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The clinical trial showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale in the intervention group compared to the standard of care group. Statistically significant pain reduction was observed at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068), and t4 (approximately 6 weeks post-surgery; p = 0.01). Significant symptom reduction was noted at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036), and t6 (p = 0.0377). The KOOS subscales for quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009), and sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

Secondary outcomes included improvements in SF-12 MCS scores (intervention: 10.8, 95% CI: 5.9 to 15.7; control: 0.8, 95% CI: -4.0 to 5.6) and reductions in ESS scores (intervention: -2.3, 95% CI: -6.5 to 1.9; control: 0.5, 95% CI: -3.6 to 4.6).

Emergency department visits reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27, 0.82; P=0.008). Hospitalizations reduced by 21% (IRR: 0.79; 95% CI 0.46, 1.35; P=0.389). Hospital outpatient visits reduced by 13% (IRR: 0.87; 95% CI 0.66, 1.14; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits increased by 2% (IRR: 1.02; 95% CI 0.73, 1.44; P=0.903). Office visits increased by 2% (IRR: 1.02; 95% CI 0.92, 1.14; P=0.672). Sleep aid medication usage dropped 18.5% (52.4% pre-index vs 42.7% post-index). Average number of prescriptions decreased from 3.98 pre-index to 3.73 post-index (P=0.552). Total two-year cost reduction post-index vs pre-index was $510,678, or -$2059 per patient.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the ViViRA digital health application (DHA) for home-based physiotherapy, while the control group received standard physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Pain (verbal rating scale, PAIN-Detect), quality of life (SF-36), mobility (BASMI) assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline: 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5]; follow-up: 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline: 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9]; follow-up: 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks.

rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise 2-3 times per week for 6 months, with assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I1 = ViViRA with personal coaching, I2 = Kaia Health with AI-based coaching, C = ViViRA without coaching
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention 1: ViViRA with personal coaching (one-time, 30-min online session); Intervention 2: Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (video-based AI guidance during sessions); Control: ViViRA without coaching.
rows.collected_data: Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASM, range: 0-10, lower scores = better mobility), PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range: 0-38, higher scores = more severe pain), PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher scores = better competence), sociodemographic data, mobility assessments, adherence (2-3 weekly use of DHA).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: All groups showed significant improvement in mobility (BASM mean change -0.6 to -0.7, p < 0.001) and pain reduction (PainDETECT mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6, p ≤ 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased significantly only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013). No coaching strategy showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same across groups after 3 months.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control group received standard physiotherapy with 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
rows.collected_data: Pain intensity assessed using a verbal numerical rating scale. Clinical relevance of pain reduction assessed using thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the verbal numerical rating scale and a pain reduction of at least 30%.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The mean difference in pain scores between the groups at 12 weeks was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. The intervention group achieved a clinically relevant reduction in pain with a mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5). The control group did not achieve clinical relevance with a mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) points or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates were 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group.

rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intra-individual control (historical findings of the same patient).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app. The control is the historical findings of the same patient.
rows.collected_data: Pain measured using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS). Functional scores were also collected as descriptive variables.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study with intraindividual control; the control is the pre-finding (historical finding) of the same patient.
rows.collected_data: Pain measured using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS); functional scores; descriptive variables including means, medians, standard deviations, changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, and Cohen's effect sizes.

rows.duration_additional_info: The study involved a twelve-week individualized home exercise program.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: A single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison of patients undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program for non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity using a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS) as the primary outcome. Secondary outcomes include self-reported, discrete-scaled functional scores on mobility, strength, and coordination. Responder analysis based on three established criteria for changes in pain intensity. Patient characteristics related to treatment adherence were also collected.

rows.duration_additional_info: Observation period lasted 12 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores using a verbal numerical rating scale; self-reported function scores.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test, T3628=5308; P<.001). Changes were clinically relevant. Function scores showed a generally positive but variable response across pain areas (back, hip, knee).

rows.duration_additional_info: Post hoc analyses indicate a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores, functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), adherence rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high attrition rate was observed.

rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities, use profiles (high use, intermediate use, low use, sub-low use), thresholds for clinically relevant improvements.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.

studies.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements across all secondary outcomes, effects stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events reported.

studies.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted nine weeks, and follow-up assessments were conducted six months later.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received immediate access to the web-based intervention Deprexis, while the control arm was a wait-list group that received access to Deprexis after six months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention arm received access to the computerized self-help program for depression (Deprexis) for 8 weeks. The control arm was a wait-list control, where participants waited 8 weeks before accessing the program.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16) administered every 2 weeks during treatment; Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) completed pre- and post-treatment; Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS) completed pre- and post-treatment; Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ) completed pre- and post-treatment; Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ) completed pre-treatment; Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ) completed pre-treatment; Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) completed pre- and post-treatment.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Unguided self-help group received a web-based self-help program without therapist support. Guided self-help group received the same program with weekly therapist e-mail feedback. Wait-list group received access to the program after 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory - II (BDI-II), Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI), Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (64-item IIP), WHOQOL-BREF, CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Symptoms of depression and other aspects were assessed over a one-year period. Controls received online self-help after the one-year follow-up.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received online self-help (Deprexis) with and without email support. The control group was a waiting-list control.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Personal Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary Outcomes: Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; PHQ-9 at 6 months and 12 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received the web-based intervention Deprexis, consisting of ten online modules with cognitive-behavioral techniques. The control arm was a wait-list group that received access to Deprexis after eight weeks.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for severity of depressive symptoms; WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) for quality of life.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed three months after randomization; secondary outcomes assessed at randomization, end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), and six months after randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = deprexis® group, C = Information group
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group used deprexis® for 90 days as an additional treatment during inpatient stay and as aftercare intervention. Placebo comparator group received weekly online information about depression for 90 days in addition to inpatient treatment and as aftercare intervention.
studies.collected_data: Reduction of depression measured with Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II) and Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9); reduction of anxiety measured with Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7); reduction of dysfunctional depression-related cognitions measured with Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS); improved self-esteem measured with Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE); improved quality of life measured with European Health Interview Survey Quality of Life-8 (EUROHIS-QOL 8); improved working ability measured with Work Ability Index (WAI); acceptance and utilization of deprexis® and placebo information measured with self-devised questionnaires; degree of childhood traumas measured with Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ); degree of structural deficits measured with OPD-Structure Questionnaire (OPD-SFK); therapeutic alliance measured with Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ); course of mood, depressiveness, and utilization of units; utilization of other treatments after inpatient treatment; satisfaction with inpatient treatment; willingness to pay; remission from depression (BDI score below cutoff of 13 and Reliable Change Index).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a stepped care model (SCM) with treatments varying by depression severity, including active monitoring, bibliotherapy, online self-help, outpatient psychotherapy, psychiatric treatment, and combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment. The control group received treatment as usual from general physicians or other health service providers.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-D-9 depression scale, cost-benefit ratio in QALYs, response/remission/relapse (PHQ-D-9), quality of life (EQ-5D, SF-12), health service utilization and medication consumption, Rosenberg self-esteem scale, Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), PHQ-D anxiety scale, PHQ-D panic symptoms, PHQ-D somatisation, GAD-7 for general anxiety disorder.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: an internet-delivered psychosocial treatment for depressive symptoms and related functional impairment. Treatment-as-Usual: Access to standard non-study care.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report; QIDS-SR-16) to measure depressive symptom severity (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16). World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS) to assess functional disability across 7 domains (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16). The Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) to measure symptom-related disability (Baseline, Post-Treatment at week 8, Follow-up at week 16).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants will be assessed at baseline (T0), mid-intervention (4 weeks, T1), post-intervention (8 weeks, T2), and follow-up (3 months post-intervention, T3).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 160 (80 in each experimental group) and C = 80
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group: Online self-help program for depression (Deprexis) for 8 weeks. Second Experimental group: Online self-help program (Deprexis) plus peer-support via an online forum for 8 weeks. Control group: Waitlist control group with access to the program after 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Depressive symptoms measured with PHQ-9 at post-treatment (8 weeks). Secondary Outcomes: Reward responses assessed using Fribourg Reward Task, SHAPS, BIS/BAS at baseline, mid-intervention (4 weeks), post-intervention (8 weeks), and follow-up (3 months). Perceived social support measured with PSS at baseline and post-treatment. Exploratory Outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7), self-efficacy (GSES), quality of life (SF-12), self-esteem (RSE), stress (PSS), trauma (CTQ-SF), pain (SSS-8), PTSD symptoms (PCL-5), client satisfaction (CSQ), usability (SUS), and working alliance.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at 3 months. Long-term online follow-up was conducted at 6 and 12 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 186 (Deprexis and DeprexisPlus combined) and C = 93 (Waitlist Control)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The trial had three arms: (1) Deprexis group received access to the web-based Deprexis program, an online tool based on principles of cognitive behavioral therapy; (2) DeprexisPlus group received the web-based Deprexis program plus scheduled email contact (1x/week); (3) Waitlist Control group waited for access to the Deprexis program for 6 months, after which they received full access to Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (Month 0 to Month 3). Secondary Outcomes: WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) with 4 subscales (Physical, Psychological, Social Relationships, Environmental) (Month 0 to Month 3); Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS) with 2 subscales (Physical and Psychological) (Month 0 to Month 3); Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC) with total score and 2 subscales (Motor and Cognitive) (Month 0 to Month 3); Chalder Fatigue Scale (Month 0 to Month 3). Other Outcomes: Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS) with 3 subtests (SMDT total score, CVLT-II learning score and delayed recall, BVMT-R learning score and delayed recall) (Month 0 to Month 3); Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R) (Month 3); Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview, clinician rating, Version 5.0.0, dichotomous variable "Major Depressive Episode, Current (yes/no)" (Month 0 to Month 3).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: Online self-help; CAU: Care as usual
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items (PHQ-9) measured from Baseline to post (3 months). Secondary Outcomes: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms - Self-Report (QIDS-SR16), Generalized Anxiety Disorder - 7 (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire - 15 items (PHQ-15), Short Form Health Survey - 12 (SF-12) measured at Baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group used the Deprexis program for 12 weeks, while the control group did not have access to the program.
studies.collected_data: Depression severity: PHQ-9; Quality of life: SF12 and EQ-5D; Social functioning: WSAS; Cost data from DAK (excluding ambulant costs); Satisfaction with DAK services and acceptance of Deprexis program.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received an 8-week internet-based program ('Deprexis') focused on depression treatment, including modules based on cognitive-behavioral therapy. Wait-list control group did not receive additional treatment during the intervention period but gained access to 'Deprexis' after post-assessment.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 (depressive symptoms), SOGS (pathological gambling), PG-YBOCS (severity of gambling symptoms), GAD-7 (anxiety levels), WSQ (common mental disorders, baseline only), subjective appraisal of intervention (post-assessment only). Demographic and psychopathological information, clinical anamnesis (e.g., current treatments, medication use, psychiatric diagnoses) were also collected.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline (T1) and after 12 weeks (T4) for all groups. Additional interim assessments were conducted at 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3) for the supported intervention group (IG-A).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: IG-A: Supported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® and brief therapeutic consultations at 4, 8, and 12 weeks. IG-B: Unsupported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® only. CG: Waitlist control group receiving no intervention during the 12-week period but gaining access to DiGA deprexis® afterward.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Reduction in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9), anxiety symptoms (GAD-7), and general psychological distress (SCL-90-R). Secondary outcomes: Acceptance (e.g., satisfaction, perceived fit), usage intensity, adherence, dropout rates, reasons for dropout, subjectively perceived effects and burdens. Exploratory outcomes: Comparison of supported vs unsupported DiGA use, moderator and predictor variables (e.g., sociodemographics, baseline symptoms, attitudes toward digital health applications), and evaluation of DiGA's relevance as a bridging intervention.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus web-based online platform (deprexis). Control group: Psychotherapy without Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) somatic symptom module, Short-Form Health Survey-12 (SF-12), Questionnaire for the evaluation of psychotherapeutic progress (FEP-2), Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ), Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Psychological empowerment scale (adapted version), Depression diagnosis by licensed psychotherapists.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at three time points: before receiving access to the treatment, after 10 weeks, and after 6 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One group received immediate access to the web-based treatment deprexis, while the other group received access after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Depression levels by BDI-II, clinical depression by Patient Health Questionnaire and SCID interview; stress perception by Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ); social support by ENRICHD Social Support Inventory (ESSI); quality of life by WHOQOL-BREF; health behavior; client satisfaction by Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received access to the Deprexis® program, consisting of ten interactive modules completed over nine weeks. The control group did not receive access to the program during the first phase.
studies.collected_data: German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation), subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items. Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis; control group was a waitlist.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS) assessing symptoms such as sadness, indecision, insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved sadness and indecision, which were associated with changes in other symptoms like early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. Most symptoms showed decreases compared to the waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons, except for agitation and early insomnia. Six somatic symptoms were not impacted by Deprexis compared to the waitlist.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Post-discharge, patients were followed by structured telephone interviews up to study-endpoint, i.e., 12 weeks after deprexis-initiation.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: TAU group received treatment-as-usual, while TAU-PLUS group received treatment-as-usual plus guided deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Beck-Depression-Inventory-II (BDI-II), Hamilton-Depression-Scale, Clinical-Global-Impression-Severity, WHO-Well-Being-Index, Helping-Alliance-Questionnaire, working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, utilization of post-hospital care after discharge.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, TAU-PLUS patients showed significant improvement in BDI-II scores (p=.03, d=-.73), greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70), earlier discharge from inpatient treatment (p=.003), lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01), and fewer re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures and alliance with therapists were not significantly different between groups. Working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Follow-up assessments were conducted at three months and nine months post-treatment.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a 12-week online intervention (deprexis), while the control group received a control condition.
studies.collected_data: Statutory health insurance costs (primary outcome), depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning (secondary outcomes). Self-reports were collected at baseline, post-treatment, and three-months and nine-months follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a 32% reduction in statutory health insurance costs (from 3139€ to 2119€ per year) compared to a 13% reduction in the control group. The intervention group also had significantly greater reductions in depression severity and impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants in the control group received delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks, and the intervention group had access to Deprexis for 90 days.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days. Control group received treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM), perceived self-efficacy measure, login frequency, dropout rates, satisfaction levels.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the intervention group showed significant improvement in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9; Cohen d=0.80; P<.001), general psychological state (CORE-OM; Cohen d=0.82; P<.001), and perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission rates were 21% in the intervention group compared to 7% in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the intervention group, and participants reported medium to high satisfaction with Deprexis.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received an 8-week course of Deprexis immediately; control group was waitlisted for 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR), interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, depression-related disability, symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for secondary outcomes such as interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy alone.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 12 weeks; secondary outcomes included anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life at 12 weeks and six months follow-up; working alliance assessed at six and 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks, with medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07-.55). No negative side effects, such as a lower working alliance, were observed in the blended format.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received 9 weeks of immediate-program-access as an add-on to treatment-as-usual. Control group received 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for depression severity as the primary outcome measure. Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) for social functioning as the secondary outcome measure. Measurements were taken at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in depression severity (BDI) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94) and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95) at 9 weeks. Improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses showed significant effects on depression (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55) and social functioning (WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). Higher percentage of intervention group experienced significant reduction in depression symptoms (BDI: OR = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, CI 95% = 2.3 - 130). Over 80% of users found the program helpful.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy only.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 3 months (primary outcome). Secondary outcomes include changes in anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, working alliance, and feasibility/acceptability of the treatment condition. studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) to measure depressive symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The sham group showed a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores, while the TILS group showed a 56% reduction, representing an additional 30% reduction in depressive symptoms with TILS. No adverse effects were reported.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
