studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS), South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for gambling-related symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive and gambling-related symptoms compared to the control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. Deprexis was particularly beneficial for individuals with high baseline gambling-related symptoms and those gambling due to loneliness.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Intermediary and long-term assessments were conducted over 36 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the Internet-based intervention (Deprexis), while the control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Symptom-specific effects of depression, quality of life aspects (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), baseline scores predicting symptom reduction, intermediary and long-term assessments, network intervention analysis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Internet interventions for depression (Deprexis) are effective in the short term and exert long-term effects. Effects are symptom-specific, directly affecting some symptoms (e.g., worthlessness, fatigue) and indirectly affecting others (e.g., depressed mood, concentration, activity level). Patients with specific problems are likely to benefit more.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week web-based cognitive behavioral intervention (deprexis) in addition to care as usual. Control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, direct health care costs. Outcomes were assessed via patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between groups. Outpatient treatment costs showed numeric differences, with psychotherapeutic treatment costs decreasing in the intervention group (-16.8% (€80)) and increasing in the control group (+14.7% (€60)) (tdf=685=2.57; p=0.008).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the Deprexis online cognitive behavioural therapy programme; control group was a waitlist control.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) scores, occurrence of depressive symptoms above clinical cutoff (BDI >13), occurrence of suicidal ideation.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group, with a mean group difference of -4.02 points (95% CI -7.26 to -0.79, p=0.015, effect size d=0.53). Worsening of depressive symptoms above the clinical cutoff occurred in 7% of the control group and 0% of the Deprexis group. No adverse events related to suicidal ideation were noted.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.collected_data: Depression symptoms pre and post intervention, patient characteristics, self-rating screening for mental disorders, attitudes towards online interventions, quality of life, adherence metrics (usage time and number of sessions).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher adherence to the program predicted greater symptom reduction in depressive symptoms over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14). Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Receiving guidance had a stronger association with symptom reduction (β = .27 and .26).

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores, indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. No sociodemographic variables were found to moderate treatment outcomes. Deprexis was equally beneficial across all subgroups.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis. The moderating effect was statistically significant (p < .05) but accounted for less than 1% of the variance in depressive symptom changes and showed limited reproducibility.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I), Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8), Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The results suggested a two-factor solution, with a very good model fit and evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and the sub-scales. The WAI-I was found to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.collected_data: Baseline variables, early change patterns in the first four weeks, latent classes (early response after registration, early response after screening, early deterioration), predictors associated with remission onset, Stepwise Cox Proportional Hazard Multiple Regression analysis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Early response after registration and early response after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission compared to early deterioration. Early improvement in depression symptoms predicted long-term remission.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants showing high impairment, improvement and low dropout probability (C3, N = 134) had the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up. Rapid improvement may lead to dropout as a result of a perceived 'good enough' dosage of treatment.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis (online intervention for depression) and control group received care as usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Compared to Millennials, Boomers used the intervention significantly more often (d = 0.45) and for a longer duration (d = 0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d = 0.14). No significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. Post-assessment between-group effect sizes for Millennials and Boomers were d = 0.26 and d = 0.39, respectively, and were stable at follow-up (d = 0.37 and d = 0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and comparably effective among Millennials and Baby Boomers.

studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) assessing nine depressive symptoms, treatment condition, changes in symptoms, network estimation techniques.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Seven of nine depressive symptoms showed larger improvements in the intervention group compared to care as usual, with effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31. No significant differences were found for the other two symptoms. Network estimation suggested that four symptom-specific effects were direct, while three were indirect. Exploratory analyses indicated greater effectiveness for participants with higher scores on the four directly affected symptoms.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014), while negative attitudes were associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). The magnitude and direction of attitude change during the trial were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). Post-assessment between-group effect size in favor of the intervention was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64), stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). Rate of participants experiencing minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%) with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The clinical trial showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale in the intervention group compared to the standard of care group. Significant symptom reduction was noted at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036), and t6 (p = 0.0377). The KOOS subscales for quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009), and sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Pain reduction was significantly larger in re.flex than in usual care, with an adjusted mean difference of 4.8 points (95% CI 0.7-8.9; P=.02; Cohen d=0.35). Improvement in physical function was not statistically significant (β=3.9 points, 95% CI 0.0-7.9, P=.049). Adherence to exercise sessions was 77% (2705/3528). A total of 12 adverse events were linked to re.flex, none of which were serious.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Statistically significant effects in favor of the IG for pain reduction (P<.001; ES=0.76), improvements in physical function (P<.001; ES=0.64), symptoms (P=.01; ES=0.53), sport and recreation activities (P=.02; ES=0.47), knee-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.76), and the physical component of general health-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.74). studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.collected_data: Change in post-stroke depression (PHQ-2), activity and participation (IMET), adherence to therapy and medication (MMAS-8), health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16), self-efficacy (ASKU), health-related quality of life (SOEP-SF12v2), and amount of recurrent strokes. studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR

studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured with PHQ-9 at 12 and 24 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Insomnia severity (ISI), daytime sleepiness (ESS), fatigue (FSS), quality of life (WHQOL_BREF), emotional state (PANAS), sleep continuity (sleep diary), self-efficacy (PCS), and emotion regulation (RSQ) at 12 and 24 weeks post-randomization.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were collected at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization.

studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Sleep continuity (sleep diary and actigraphy, continuously for 8 weeks), Dysfunctional Beliefs About Sleep (DBAS, at 8 and 16 weeks), Pre-Sleep Arousal (PSAS, at 8 and 16 weeks), Daytime well-being (WHO-5, at 8 and 16 weeks), Quality of life (WHQOL_BREF, at 8 and 16 weeks), Symptoms of anxiety and depression (HADS, at 8 and 16 weeks).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) were observed, indicating robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital CBT-I arm compared to the control group at 8 and 16 weeks post-randomization. studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia delivered via the "Somnio" app in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Arm 2: Filling in of sleep-diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic.
studies.collected_data: Posttraumatic symptoms assessed with PCL-5 and PTCI questionnaires. Sleep quality measured with sleep diaries and PSQI. Anxiety assessed with GAD-7, depression with QIDS-SR16, and life quality with WHODAS 2.0. Measurements conducted prior to intervention, at 10 weeks, and at 6 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR

Secondary Outcomes: German Pain Questionnaire (DSF), Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI), Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS), Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS), Opinions About Sleep Questionnaire (DBAS), WHO-5 Well-Being Index (WHO-5), WHO Quality of Life (WHOQOL_BREF), Basic Psychological Need Satisfaction and Frustration Scale (BPNSFS), Mannheim Dream Questionnaire (MADRE), Nightmare Distress Questionnaire (NDQ), General Depression Scale (ADS-K), State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI), Questionnaire for Analysis of Motivational Schemas (FAMOS), Incongruence Questionnaire (K-INK) (Baseline, 8 and 24 weeks).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR

studies.collected_data: ISI Insomnia Severity Index, DBAS-16 Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep, FSS Fatigue Severity Index, ESS Epworth Sleepiness Scale, CES-D Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (short version), STAI-T State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, WHOQOL_BREF WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire (brief version), WHO-5 WHO-Five Well-Being Index, MADRE Mannheim Dream Questionnaire, DTI Düsseldorf Dream Inventory, NDQ Nightmare Distress Questionnaire, CAN Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares. studies.key_outcomes_findings: At posttreatment, dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI (Diffadj = -7.60) compared to WLC (d = -2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26–1.02) and at long-term follow-up (intervention group only; ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received digital CBT-I via a mobile app or web platform (10 sequential core modules based on CBT-I techniques). Control group was a waiting list control (WLC).
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, mechanistic effect measures.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects at 12- and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (-3.34 and -2.83; ps <0.001; ds = 0.66-0.78) in the dCBT-I group. Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46-1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33-1.14). Effects remained stable across a heterogeneous sample.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR

Pain-related impairments and life control also improved (ds = 0.22-0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were observed for secondary outcomes, including daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47-1.12).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

Movement and gait analysis with sensors. Measurement of BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, PAHCO. Clinical routine: serology blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, physical examination, bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP). Measurement of hand strength.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed significant improvement in mobility (BASMI score: baseline 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5], follow-up 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4], p=0.05), while the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9], follow-up 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2], p=0.00). The intervention group also demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5±2.8) compared to the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5±2) after 12 weeks.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise 2-3 times per week for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: ViViRA with personal coaching included a one-time, 30-min online coaching session prior to using DHA. Kaia Health with AI-based coaching provided video-based AI movement guidance during each session. The control group used ViViRA without coaching.
studies.collected_data: Sociodemographic data, questionnaires, mobility (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index - BASMI), pain intensity (PainDETECT), and Physical Activity-related Health Competence (PAHCO). Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions significantly improved mobility (BASMI: mean change -0.6 to -0.7, p < 0.001) and reduced pain intensity (PainDETECT: mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points, p ≤ 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased significantly only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013). No coaching strategy showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same across groups after 3 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group achieved a mean pain score reduction of -3.35 (SD 2.05) or -53.1% (SD 29.5), which was clinically relevant. The control group had a mean pain score reduction of -0.91 (SD 1.5) or -14.6% (SD 25.3), which did not reach clinical relevance. The mean difference in pain scores between the groups at 12 weeks was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (T3628=5308; P<.001), within the range of clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive but more variable response across pain areas.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Post hoc analyses indicate a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores, functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), adherence rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high attrition rate was observed.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities, use profiles (high use, intermediate use, low use, sub-low use), clinically relevant pain improvement thresholds.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.

studies.duration_additional_info: The Caterna visual training is conducted for 90 days (13 weeks) daily for 30-45 minutes. Prospective data analysis will occur over a period of 5 years.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The study involves amblyopia therapy using the Caterna online visual training, which is used in addition to occlusion therapy. The Caterna visual training games are played on the computer for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Visual acuity improvement in the worse eye until it matches the better eye or no further improvement is observed. Secondary Outcome: Optimization of amblyopia therapy through data analysis of a nationwide network. Data includes regular check-ups by orthoptists and ophthalmologists, and adherence to Caterna visual training (30-45 minutes daily for 90 days).

studies.duration_additional_info: The study analyzed children with at least 90 days of prior occlusion therapy before intervention.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The FAVAS group showed a significant improvement in BCVA, with a mean gain of 0.15 logMAR (95% CI [0.11;0.18], p < 0.001), compared to a non-significant improvement of 0.01 logMAR (95% CI [-0.02;0.04], p = 0.356) in the control group. Opioid Consumption: Standard of Care Group median change = -28.0 MME (IQR -35.0 to -23.5), Standard of Care + RelieVRx Group median change = -34.8 MME (IQR -37.0 to -23.5). KOOS Jr. Score: Standard of Care Group median change = 27.6 (IQR 17.5 to 38.0), Standard of Care + RelieVRx Group median change = 32.2 (IQR 21.2 to 42.0). VR-12 Physical Component Score: Standard of Care Group median change = 7.2 (IQR 2.0 to 11.4), Standard of Care + RelieVRx Group median change = 9.5 (IQR 4.0 to 12.8).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA

studies.key_outcomes_findings: RelieVRx was superior to Sham for reductions in pain intensity (difference: 0.406 [0.170-0.642]) and pain interference (difference: 0.523 [0.285-0.760]). Clinically meaningful reductions were observed for RelieVRx at day 56: pain intensity reduced by 2.0 points [1.73-2.06] and pain interference reduced by 2.3 points [1.99-2.33].

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Pretreatment to 12-month posttreatment analyses were conducted.

More than half of Skills-Based VR participants reported at least a 2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both at 12 months posttreatment.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a culturally adapted digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) program (Somryst) along with usual care and a sleep hygiene brochure. The control group received minimally enhanced usual care (mEUC), which included standard care by their primary care provider, potentially including pharmacotherapy, and a sleep hygiene brochure.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Change in Mean Insomnia Severity Index Score (ISI) from baseline to post-intervention (9 weeks) and baseline to 6 months post-intervention. Secondary outcomes: Change in Mean Quality of Life Score (Short-Form 12 [SF-12], MCS), Change in Mean Daytime Sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale [ESS]), Change in Mean Satisfaction With Care (Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care [PACIC]). Other outcomes: Change in Mean Sleep Quality (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index [PSQI]), Change in Mean Self-reported Wake After Sleep Onset (14-day sleep diary), Change in Mean Actigraphy Assessed Wake After Sleep Onset (14-day wrist-actigraphy), Change in Mean Self-reported Sleep Onset Latency (14-day sleep diary).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a greater reduction in ISI scores (-6.7, 95% CI: -15.4 to 2.0) compared to the control group (-3.9, 95% CI: -12.5 to 4.7) at 9 weeks. At 6 months, the intervention group also showed a greater reduction (-5.7, 95% CI: -17.1 to 5.8) compared to the control group (-1.6, 95% CI: -13.0 to 9.9). Secondary outcomes included improvements in SF-12 MCS scores (intervention: 10.8, 95% CI: 5.9 to 15.7; control: 0.8, 95% CI: -4.0 to 5.6) and reductions in ESS scores (intervention: -2.3, 95% CI: -6.5 to 1.9; control: 0.5, 95% CI: -3.6 to 4.6).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) for mobile-delivered CBT-I (Somryst); control group did not receive the PDT.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index score, healthcare utilisation, health utility scores, clinical outcomes, sleep outcomes (measured with sleep diaries), individual patient-reported outcomes (depressive symptoms, daytime sleepiness, health status, stress, anxiety), engagement with the PDT (for intervention group).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: SHUTi: digital CBT-I intervention; HealthWatch: attention-matched control internet program
studies.collected_data: Online sleep diaries measuring sleep-onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), sleep efficiency (SE), number of awakenings, sleep quality, and total sleep time (TST)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the SHUTi condition had greater improvements at posttest compared with control for SOL, WASO, SE, number of awakenings, and sleep quality (p < .02 for all outcomes except TST). Statistically significant increases in TST were observed only at 12- and 18-months. Improvements were sustained at every follow-up.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.collected_data: Event type, patient demographics, outcomes, product details.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Two adverse event reports were identified: one involving Somryst for insomnia prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder, resulting in disability; the other involving EndeavorRx, reporting perceived ineffectiveness without clinical harm. No adverse events were identified for the remaining 11 PDTs.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants completed assessments at baseline, before completing treatment cores, immediately post-intervention, and at 6-month and 1-year follow-ups.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants accessed the FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (Somryst®) delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia via six interactive treatment cores and daily sleep diaries.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), 8-item Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-8), Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 scale (GAD-7), daily sleep diaries.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ISI scores decreased from 18.8 to 9.9 (P <.001) for those completing all 6 cores, with sustained reductions at immediate post (11.0), 6-month (11.6), and 1-year follow-ups (12.2) (P <.001). PHQ-8 and GAD-7 scores also showed significant decreases at all measured timepoints (P <.001). 58% of participants were adherent, and 46.8% completed all 6 cores.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) scores, healthcare resource utilization (HCRU) data (emergency department visits, hospitalizations, hospital outpatient visits, ambulatory surgical center visits, office visits), sleep aid medication usage, number of prescriptions, and cost data.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Mean ISI score declined by 37.2% from baseline (19.1 vs 12.0). 58.8% of patients achieved ISI responder status (ISI score improved by =>7; NNT: 1.7). 26.6% of patients achieved insomnia remission (ISI score <8; NNT for remission: 3.8). Emergency department visits reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27, 0.82; P=0.008). Hospitalizations reduced by 21% (IRR: 0.79; 95% CI 0.46, 1.35; P=0.389). Hospital outpatient visits reduced by 13% (IRR: 0.87; 95% CI 0.66, 1.14; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits increased by 2% (IRR: 1.02; 95% CI 0.73, 1.44; P=0.903). Office visits increased by 2% (IRR: 1.02; 95% CI 0.92, 1.14; P=0.672). Sleep aid medication usage dropped 18.5% (52.4% pre-index vs 42.7% post-index). Average number of prescriptions decreased from 3.98 pre-index to 3.73 post-index (P=0.552). Total two-year cost reduction post-index vs pre-index was $510,678, or -$2059 per patient.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Average duration of the program was 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Urinary Impact Questionnaire-short form (UIQ-7), Pelvic Floor Impact Questionnaire (PFIQ-7), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7), Work Productivity and Activity Impairment questionnaire (WPAI), intention to seek additional healthcare, engagement, satisfaction.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant improvement on UIQ-7 (8.8, 95%CI 4.7; 12.9, p < 0.001) with a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements observed in all secondary outcomes and high satisfaction (8.9/10, SD 1.8).

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: Smartphone app ViViRA Neck with access for 12 weeks. Control group: Standard of care, i.e., at least 1 prescription with 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Pain intensity measured using Visual Analogue Scale (VAS, 0-100). Secondary Outcomes: Disability measured using Neck Disability Index (NDI) and quality of life measured using Veterans Rand 12-item Health Survey (VR12).

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the ViViRA digital health app and exercised for 12 weeks, 3 times weekly for 20 minutes, with additional physical activity measured by a wristband sensor. Control group underwent physiotherapy once weekly as standard care.
studies.collected_data: Clinical disease markers (BASMI, BASFI, BASDAI, ASDAS, ASAS 20), physical activity via activity sensor, movement and gait analysis with sensors, BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, PAHCO, serology blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, physical examination, bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP), hand strength measurement.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app. Arm 2: Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists.
studies.collected_data: Pain reduction measured by VNRS after 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Function improvement of mobility.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the ViViRA digital health application (DHA) for home-based physiotherapy, while the control group received standard physiotherapy.
studies.collected_data: Pain (verbal rating scale, PAIN-Detect), quality of life (SF-36), mobility (BASMI) assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline: 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5]; follow-up: 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline: 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9]; follow-up: 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks.

studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise 2-3 times per week for 6 months, with assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I1 = ViViRA with personal coaching, I2 = Kaia Health with AI-based coaching, C = ViViRA without coaching
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention 1: ViViRA with personal coaching (one-time, 30-min online session); Intervention 2: Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (video-based AI guidance during sessions); Control: ViViRA without coaching.
studies.collected_data: Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASM, range: 0-10, lower scores = better mobility), PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range: 0-38, higher scores = more severe pain), PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher scores = better competence), sociodemographic data, mobility assessments, adherence (2-3 weekly use of DHA).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: All groups showed significant improvement in mobility (BASM mean change -0.6 to -0.7, p < 0.001) and pain reduction (PainDETECT mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6, p ≤ 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased significantly only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013). No coaching strategy showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same across groups after 3 months.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control group received standard physiotherapy with 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
studies.collected_data: Pain intensity assessed using a verbal numerical rating scale. Clinical relevance of pain reduction assessed using thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the verbal numerical rating scale and a pain reduction of at least 30%.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The mean difference in pain scores between the groups at 12 weeks was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. The intervention group achieved a clinically relevant reduction in pain with a mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5). The control group did not achieve clinical relevance with a mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) points or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates were 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group.

studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intra-individual control (historical findings of the same patient).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app. The control is the historical findings of the same patient.
studies.collected_data: Pain measured using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS). Functional scores were also collected as descriptive variables.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study with intraindividual control; the control is the pre-finding (historical finding) of the same patient.
studies.collected_data: Pain measured using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS); functional scores; descriptive variables including means, medians, standard deviations, changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, and Cohen's effect sizes.

studies.duration_additional_info: The study involved a twelve-week individualized home exercise program.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: A single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison of patients undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program for non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity using a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS) as the primary outcome. Secondary outcomes include self-reported, discrete-scaled functional scores on mobility, strength, and coordination. Responder analysis based on three established criteria for changes in pain intensity. Patient characteristics related to treatment adherence were also collected.

studies.duration_additional_info: Observation period lasted 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores using a verbal numerical rating scale; self-reported function scores.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test, T3628=5308; P<.001). Changes were clinically relevant. Function scores showed a generally positive but variable response across pain areas (back, hip, knee).

studies.duration_additional_info: Post hoc analyses indicate a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores, functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), adherence rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high attrition rate was observed.

studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities, use profiles (high use, intermediate use, low use, sub-low use), thresholds for clinically relevant improvements.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.
